So the context of the assignment was to kind of create a framework and playbooks within the four different events, event types.
That includes a CMO breakfast and executive dinner series, industry happy hours, and annual summit.

And in my plan and playbook, I'm thinking through how do we select the audience, how do we promote it pre-show and post-show, and what do we do for, like, the day of logistics, and what are some of our activations for each one of these programs.
I normally like to kind of lay out instead of doing events ad hoc of, like, okay, this month we want to do one happy hour, one dinner, and then maybe, like, consider, like, a workshop here.
There for some of our meetups, things like that.
And I think I have to kind of think through like each one of these event types, like what is going to be our target audience and how do we kind of select for like each one of these events?
So a lot of times at Notion, we look at tier one and tier two cities.
Where are the accounts sitting in terms of geographic locations?
Most of our tier one events have been in San Francisco and then some in New York as well.
We started looking into doing LA.
There's some movement there and like some founders as well.
And then tier two cities like Seattle, Austin, and Chicago.
I've also done meetups there.
And those are definitely up and coming cities that I would like to kind of experiment and test in for across like some of these events.
And then in terms of like the event types, you know, these are just some key metrics I would like.

The cadence would be like once or twice a month, as I mentioned, for SEMO breakfasts and dinners, happy hours, once a quarter, paired alongside if there's a conference in town with the right target audience, and then the once a year annual summit, where it's going to be around 100 folks.
There's, you know, a budget that I probably run like 10k to spend.
And then kind of what are some activations, workshop topics, speakers, partners that we can kind of think through for that summit.

then, based on what people want to know, we can kind of shape the topics around it too during the breakfast.

There are somewhat champions as well of the product.

And then finalize catering, map out the layout of the room, order like kind of event logistical things from Amazon, and then finalize discussion guides with Marcel if you are going to be hosting, kind of go through the workshop details and the content behind it.

So we would do the content prep and dry run two weeks before, and then one week before, prepare all the name tags, finalize all the registrations, send finalized confirmation, and like logistics as well.

Day of, I usually like to get into the room an hour before, I would probably want four or five volunteers from the team to kind of just help me run like the day of, someone be an usher, someone be in charge of the food station, somebody help me with check-in and greeting guests, and then test out the AV equipment a few hours before, if that is something.

That we need for presentations post event I like to send thank you emails within 48 hours and NPS survey to all attendees to get feedback on what they liked about the content and then any resources like templates, guides that came out of the workshop as along with photos and then personalize any follow ups that might have raised a hand or asked some questions to qualify them as And then a week after post show, analyze like what are some of the engagement metrics that we have in the funnel, document any lessons learned, pretty much create like a one pager of things that went well and then things that we could improve on and then follow up schedule up meetings with like interested prospects with the sales team.
So that's pretty much like the run of the show and like the playbook that I would do for like the CMO breakfast track.

think it's, like, a number you shared.

Like, how do you kind of manage that when, like, you know, some of them might not even show up, I guess?
Yeah.
So a lot of these are, like, the person to the left and right of you, it's usually the same target, like, the same title of people or, like, the same size or same industry.
So I like to group people together that way.
Usually about 30 minutes before dinner starts, if I see there's a name card that no one's sitting at.
I usually take it away and then ask anyone that's like trickling in to see if they want to be like still sat at the same table or like move to the other side.
Because there are like new school chairs sometimes, especially if you deal with like, oh, like someone like knows someone in the room that they didn't know was coming.
So I usually am OK making those switches as long as like the other person that their name card is there, they're OK switching to.
So that's not really an issue.
It's something I keep an eye on pretty actively throughout the check-in process, which is like the first hour of the night.

Yeah, would you be able to go through your thoughts on that one?
Yeah, so in terms of like the event formatting, it's going to be a full day.
Three to six, and then plus like an evening reception paired with it, that's going to be the format of the summit.
Some options I've worked at in terms of putting together summits is like Shack 15, Terra Gala, the Pearl.
Just because like these hit all our AV needs, it hits like the spacing requirement as well if we're going to be going for 150 to 200 growth leaders.

And then in terms of like the breakdown, we would have like the keynote on the main stage, a breakfast, and then the midday was like would be kind of starting with like the case study panels and then like just story panels in general between like maybe two or three CMOs or co-founders.
It would pretty much lead into interactive workshops and breakout sessions.
Some of the content here I've covered is like around AI, how to build a like high-performing growth team, turning content.
And subscribers, kind of like hashing that, those breakout sessions out in terms of topics.
And then it would close off with a happy hour of the evening, everybody's invited to.
And then from the 150 to 200 people that attend, we cherry pick about 20 CMOs to join us for a VIP dinner.
That's like invitation only.

Execution timeline, because this is a summit, it is just...
Once a year, I like to start planning things three months ahead of time, if not even sooner.
That's when we start with, like, the beginning of speaker outreaches, think preliminary about the budget that we need, sponsorships, and then developing, like, a brand and theme around it.
And, of course, like, securing the date.

Leading up to about a month out, continuing to do, like, those one-on-one outreaches, inviting people through Luma that have, like, come to past events in our space that might fit the right target audience.
Create a volunteer ask, because it is a big summit.
We probably need quite a few GrowthX employees, so I kind of create an agenda of what we need at each station.
Order a speaker, gifts, book the videography, photographer.
We probably want to capture some bite-sized content if we want to be thinking about event, like, life cycle for the summit.
Okay.

And then the day before, day of, again, running logistics, making sure we have a note before you go email that's sent out of where everything is, like what are the workshops that are available, and then post show, like send the thank you notes, the NPS survey, and then kind of create a look back doc of like what went well and what we need to improve on, and then follow up with like qualified leads there.

I think like, maybe just some, we'll have your kind of take on the relationship building piece, you know, or something that of the programs you ran and things you've done, that if you could only do one thing again in the last 12 months, what is the most impactful thing you think you did, you know, program, event?
Yeah, so in terms of event programming, webinars and workshops, virtual workshops have see
I did 19 events in the last 90 days at Notion, which is kind of crazy, but we did a look-back calendar of measuring the format and the ROI behind each one of the events.

Workshops and virtual events was number one, where it doesn't cost anything besides just time and resource, but you can reach so many people across like different channels and geography time zones by just having a workshop and then sending out like templates and a one-pager to follow up with that.
So I would say I would invest mostly in workshops because it's scalable and repeatable.
The second thing I would say is like the meetups.
I think people after COVID also really crave interaction, shaking someone's hand or just, you know, getting outside like their nine-to-five job to go to a happy hour.
People have really enjoyed that.
And it's scalable.
We are able to use some of our ambassadors.

Like if I'm not in Austin, I'm able to lean on someone on the community team to help me co-host where I offer them virtual support and logistics, create the Luma page for them, and then let them kind of run it alongside with a partner.
So that's kind of like the best combo is like a happy hour plus a partner.
You get to marry the two of them sourcing for audiences as well and targetless, and then our side too to kind of fill the room on both ends.
